title: Beckman Coulter GEM Premier 4000 / 5000 Series - Sample Aspiration Failure or Clot Detection Errors
description: Troubleshooting Beckman Coulter GEM Premier 4000 / 5000 analyzers for sample aspiration failures or clot detection errors.

steps.title: Verify Sample Collection
steps.instructions:
Ensure the sample syringe or tube is properly filled and mixed.
Check for visible clots or hemolysis.
Why: Clotted or poorly mixed samples can trigger clot detection errors.

steps.title: Inspect Sample Lines and Connectors
steps.instructions:
Examine the sample line for kinks, bends, or obstructions.
Confirm all connections are seated securely.
Why: Any restriction can prevent proper aspiration.

steps.title: Check the Sample Probe
steps.instructions:
Visually inspect the probe for residual blood, debris, or damage.
If dirty, follow manufacturer-approved cleaning procedures.
Why: Blocked or contaminated probes are a common cause of aspiration failure.

steps.title: Verify Reagent Cartridges
steps.instructions:
Ensure cartridges are installed correctly and not expired.
Check for any leaks or blockages at the cartridge interface.
Why: Faulty cartridges can interfere with aspiration and clot detection systems.

steps.title: Run a Manual Prime
steps.instructions:
If the analyzer allows, perform a manual prime to flush the sample path.
Observe if the prime completes successfully without error.
Why: Confirms the fluidics system is functional.

steps.title: Check System Alerts and Error Logs
steps.instructions:
Review analyzer messages related to aspiration pressure, clots, or fluidics.
Why: Provides insight into whether the issue is mechanical or sample-related.

steps.title: Swap Sample Lines or Probe if Available
steps.instructions:
Replace with a known good line or probe and attempt a test draw.
Why: Helps isolate whether the problem is with the hardware or the sample itself.

CCR.Complaint: Analyzer fails to aspirate sample and reports clot detection error.
CCR.Cause: Sample line was obstructed; probe had residual blood buildup causing aspiration failure.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned sample probe and replaced obstructed sample line; performed manual prime. Analyzer tested successfully with control sample.

helpfulDetails: Whether outlet power was stable
Tubing and probe condition
Error codes or alarm messages observed
Whether a manual prime succeeded
Final device status: ready for patient use or sent for repair

title: Beckman Coulter GEM Premier 4000 / 5000 Series - Sensor Cartridge or Reagent Pack Recognition Errors
description: Guide for troubleshooting GEM Premier 4000 / 5000 blood gas analyzers when sensor cartridges or reagent packs are not recognized.

steps.title: Verify Consumable Type and Expiration
steps.instructions:
Check that the sensor cartridge or reagent pack is compatible with the GEM Premier 4000 / 5000 model.
Ensure the consumable is not expired. Expired packs are often rejected by the analyzer.
Why it matters: The analyzer’s firmware will not accept incompatible or expired consumables.

steps.title: Inspect Consumable Packaging and Connectors
steps.instructions:
Remove the cartridge or reagent pack and inspect for dust, damage, or bent pins.
Ensure protective caps are removed and electrical contacts are clean.
Why it matters: Poor contact can prevent recognition even if the consumable is new.

steps.title: Confirm Proper Installation
steps.instructions:
Reinsert the consumable according to the analyzer’s alignment guides.
Listen for any click or tactile feedback indicating it’s seated correctly.
Why it matters: Misalignment is a common source of recognition errors.

steps.title: Check Analyzer Status Indicators
steps.instructions:
Observe display messages and LED indicators during insertion.
Note if the analyzer prompts for the consumable type or shows an error code.
Why it matters: Specific codes help determine if the issue is consumable-related or internal.

steps.title: Cycle Power if Needed
steps.instructions:
Power down the analyzer completely, wait 30 seconds, then power back on.
Reinsert the consumable after reboot.
Why it matters: Temporary communication errors between the analyzer and consumable may resolve after a reset.

steps.title: Try a Known Good Consumable
steps.instructions:
Replace with a consumable that is verified functional.
If recognized, the original consumable may be defective.
Why it matters: Confirms whether the problem is consumable or analyzer-related.

CCR.Complaint: Analyzer does not recognize newly installed sensor cartridge or reagent pack.
CCR.Cause: Consumable properly installed, connectors clean, firmware compatible, but analyzer continues to reject consumable.
CCR.Resolution: Performed basic troubleshooting, verified consumable, cycled power, issue persists. Analyzer removed from service and sent for repair.

helpfulDetails: Consumable lot numbers and expiration dates
Error codes displayed
LED or screen messages
Any unusual sounds during insertion
Analyzer status before and after power cycle

title: Beckman Coulter GEM Premier 4000 / 5000 Series - Automatic Calibration Failures or QC Lockouts
description: Troubleshooting Beckman Coulter GEM Premier 4000 / 5000 analyzers that report automatic calibration failures or enter QC lockouts.

steps.title: Verify Analyzer Status and Alerts
steps.instructions: Check the display for specific calibration or QC error codes. Note which QC level or cartridge triggered the lockout. Why: Understanding the error code helps narrow whether it is reagent-related, hardware, or environmental.

steps.title: Check Power and Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure the analyzer is securely connected to a reliable power source. Verify any UPS or power conditioner is functioning. Why: Unstable power can interrupt calibration sequences and trigger lockouts.

steps.title: Inspect Reagents and Cartridges
steps.instructions: Confirm that all sensor cartridges and QC/reagent packs are correctly installed and within expiration dates. Verify storage conditions (temperature, light exposure). Swap with a known good cartridge if available. Why: Expired or improperly seated reagents are the most common cause of QC/calibration failures.

steps.title: Check Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: Ensure the analyzer is within recommended operating temperature and humidity. Avoid direct sunlight or drafts near the analyzer. Why: Temperature extremes can prevent proper calibration.

steps.title: Run Manual QC / Calibration
steps.instructions: Attempt a manual calibration using standard QC solutions. Observe whether the error persists or clears. Why: This can help differentiate between software/calibration issues and physical reagent problems.

steps.title: Verify Software and Connectivity
steps.instructions: Confirm the analyzer software is current and correctly communicating with LIS if applicable. Check that QC schedules match those configured in the analyzer. Why: Software mismatches or misconfigured schedules can result in lockouts even if reagents are valid.

CCR.Complaint: Analyzer will not complete automatic calibration and is locked out from patient testing.
CCR.Cause: QC lockout triggered; inspection shows expired QC solution and one reagent pack not seated properly.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced expired QC solution, reseated reagent pack, reran manual calibration; analyzer successfully cleared lockout.

helpfulDetails: Error codes observed.
QC solution lot numbers and expiration dates.
Reagent pack serial numbers and installation confirmation.
Temperature/humidity in the analyzer room.
Any prior similar lockouts noted.

title: Beckman Coulter GEM Premier 4000 / 5000 Series - Internal Pump or Fluidics Blockages
description: Guide for troubleshooting Beckman Coulter GEM Premier analyzers when internal pumps or fluidics pathways are blocked, focusing on external checks and escalation.

steps.title: Verify Power and Startup Sequence
steps.instructions:
Confirm the analyzer powers on normally.
Check that no startup errors or alarms indicate immediate pump failure.
Why: A pump cannot operate if the analyzer is not correctly powered or initialized.

steps.title: Inspect Sample and Reagent Paths Externally
steps.instructions:
Confirm sample lines, syringes, and reagent packs are seated and undamaged.
Look for kinks, leaks, or visible clots in tubing.
Why: External obstructions are more common and easier to fix than internal pump issues.

steps.title: Check Fluidics Waste and Drain Lines
steps.instructions:
Ensure waste lines are not full, clogged, or pinched.
Verify proper drainage from the analyzer into the waste container.
Why: Fluid backup can mimic pump failure or cause partial blockage.

steps.title: Run Diagnostic Fluidics Test (if available)
steps.instructions:
Use the analyzer’s built-in fluidics check or maintenance menu to test pump operation.
Observe flow rates and error messages carefully.
Why: Confirms whether the pump moves fluid correctly without introducing patient samples.

steps.title: Swap External Components if Applicable
steps.instructions:
Replace sample syringes, reagent packs, or tubing to rule out external contamination or clots.
Why: External component failure can appear as an internal blockage.

steps.title: Observe Alarms or Error Codes
steps.instructions:
Note specific fluidics or pump error codes.
Reference the service manual only for code interpretation, not internal disassembly.
Why: Provides evidence for escalation if the issue persists.

CCR.Complaint: Analyzer reports pump failure; sample aspiration incomplete.
CCR.Cause: External tubing and reagent packs confirmed seated; diagnostic fluidics test shows no flow through internal pump, indicating blockage.
CCR.Resolution: Analyzer removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for manufacturer repair.

helpfulDetails: Power status and startup sequence
External tubing and reagent pack inspection results
Waste line condition
Diagnostic fluidics test outcomes
Specific error codes or alarms
Any unusual sounds, heat, or smells

title: Beckman Coulter GEM Premier 4000 / 5000 Series - LIS / Network Communication Failures for Result Transmission
description: Guide to troubleshoot GEM Premier analyzers unable to transmit results to LIS or hospital network, emphasizing external checks before repair.

steps.title: Verify Power and System Status
steps.instructions: Ensure the analyzer is powered on and fully booted. Check that all status LEDs indicate normal operation. Reason: Communication may fail if the analyzer is in error or startup mode.

steps.title: Check Network Connection
steps.instructions: Confirm that the network cable is properly seated and not damaged. If using Wi-Fi (rare for GEM Premier), verify signal strength and authentication. Reason: Loose or faulty connections are the most common external cause of LIS failures.

steps.title: Ping the LIS / Host System
steps.instructions: Using another workstation on the same network, ping the LIS server IP to confirm network accessibility. Reason: Confirms that network path and IP addressing are valid.

steps.title: Verify Analyzer Network Configuration
steps.instructions: Check IP address, subnet mask, and gateway on the GEM Premier analyzer via system settings. Compare with network documentation or IT configuration for the lab. Reason: Misconfigured network settings prevent communication with LIS.

steps.title: Inspect LIS Interface Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm that the LIS interface (HL7, TCP/IP, or other protocol) is enabled and configured correctly. Ensure proper port numbers and host addresses are set. Reason: Analyzer may fail to send results if LIS interface is disabled or misconfigured.

steps.title: Check for Software or Firmware Alerts
steps.instructions: Review analyzer logs for recent communication errors or alerts. Note timestamps and error codes. Reason: Provides evidence if issue is persistent or intermittent.

steps.title: Test Result Transmission with a Sample
steps.instructions: Run a QC or patient sample and observe if results reach LIS. Document any error messages. Reason: Confirms whether the problem is fully resolved or ongoing.

CCR.Complaint: Analyzer unable to transmit results to LIS; network error displayed.
CCR.Cause: Network cable was loose and analyzer IP settings were misconfigured.
CCR.Resolution: Secured cable and updated IP settings per IT documentation; verified test sample results successfully transmitted.

helpfulDetails: Status LEDs on analyzer
Network cable inspection
IP address, subnet, gateway, and LIS host settings
Error logs with timestamps
QC / patient sample results after correction

title: Beckman Coulter iQ200 Series Urine Analyzer - Sample Aspiration / Flow Cell Blockage
description: Troubleshooting iQ200 sample aspiration, low flow, clogging, probe, tubing, or flow cell blockage symptoms before escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the iQ200 is not actively processing patient specimens before opening covers, handling sample racks, checking tubing, or initiating cleaning. If urine microscopy testing is needed immediately, have laboratory staff use another validated analyzer or follow approved downtime procedures. Expected outcome: No patient specimen is delayed, lost, or resulted from an analyzer with unresolved aspiration or flow issues.

steps.title: Stop Additional Testing
steps.instructions: Ask laboratory staff not to load additional racks or specimens until the aspiration issue is resolved. Confirm whether any specimen was in process when the error occurred and whether results were completed, flagged, rejected, or need repeat testing per lab policy. Expected outcome: Questionable or incomplete urine microscopy results are not released.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Symptom
steps.instructions: Ask staff what they observed. Check whether the issue involves: Sample aspiration error; Low sample volume message; Flow cell blockage warning; Repeated clog or obstruction message; No sample movement through tubing; Bubbles in the sample path; Carryover or abnormal image quality after aspiration; Failure only with certain specimens. Expected outcome: The issue is confirmed as an aspiration or fluid path problem, not a rack transport, barcode, LIS, or general startup issue.

steps.title: Inspect the Sample Tube or Specimen Container
steps.instructions: Confirm the specimen container is appropriate for the analyzer and contains enough urine for aspiration. Check for short sample volume, foam, heavy sediment, mucus, crystals, clots, or debris that may interfere with aspiration. Expected outcome: The sample itself is not the cause of low aspiration or flow blockage. If the issue is limited to one poor-quality specimen, have the lab repeat or process the sample according to laboratory policy and stop troubleshooting the analyzer.

steps.title: Check Sample Rack Loading and Position
steps.instructions: Verify the rack is seated correctly and the sample container is positioned properly for probe access. Look for tilted tubes, incorrect container height, caps left on, damaged racks, or anything preventing the probe from reaching the sample correctly. Expected outcome: The probe can access the sample without obstruction or misalignment.

steps.title: Inspect the Aspiration Probe Externally
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the sample probe for dried urine, residue, bent appearance, or obstruction at the probe tip. Do not force anything into the probe or attempt internal probe repair. Expected outcome: The probe appears clean, straight, and unobstructed from the accessible exterior view.

steps.title: Run the Approved Cleaning or Rinse Cycle
steps.instructions: Use the analyzer’s normal operator-accessible cleaning, rinse, or unclog routine if available and permitted by site procedure. This matters because dried urine sediment, crystals, or debris can temporarily restrict flow without indicating a failed internal component. Expected outcome: The analyzer completes the cleaning cycle without repeating the aspiration or blockage error. If the cleaning cycle clears the error and verification testing passes, return the device to service and stop.

steps.title: Check Reagent, Rinse, and Waste Containers
steps.instructions: Verify required fluids are present, connected, seated correctly, and not expired if applicable. Confirm waste is not full and that waste tubing is not kinked, pinched, disconnected, or routed uphill in a way that could restrict drainage. Expected outcome: Fluid handling is not being limited by an empty supply, full waste container, or external tubing restriction.

steps.title: Inspect External Tubing and Connections
steps.instructions: Check visible tubing for kinks, pinches, loose fittings, air gaps, crystallized residue, or leaks. Pay close attention to tubing that may have been moved during cleaning, reagent replacement, waste handling, or bench relocation. Expected outcome: The external fluid path is connected, routed correctly, and free of obvious restriction.

steps.title: Look for Leaks, Bubbles, or Abnormal Fluid Movement
steps.instructions: During a safe prime, rinse, or cleaning cycle, observe whether fluid moves consistently through visible tubing. Watch for excessive air bubbles, dripping, no fluid movement, or fluid backing up. Expected outcome: Fluid movement appears steady and controlled without visible leakage or air intrusion.

steps.title: Check Recent Maintenance or Consumable Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether the issue started after: Probe cleaning; Flow cell cleaning; Reagent or rinse replacement; Waste container emptying; Tubing movement; Analyzer relocation; Processing highly sedimented specimens. Expected outcome: Any recent change that could have introduced air, a loose connection, or a blockage is identified.

steps.title: Repeat Startup, Prime, or System Check
steps.instructions: After correcting external fluid, sample, rack, or tubing concerns, restart the analyzer or run the appropriate startup/prime/system check per facility procedure. Expected outcome: The iQ200 completes its check without aspiration, flow, or blockage errors.

steps.title: Run Quality Control or Verification Testing
steps.instructions: Before returning the analyzer to clinical use, confirm acceptable QC or verification performance according to lab policy. Expected outcome: The analyzer demonstrates acceptable aspiration and result performance before patient testing resumes.

steps.title: Return to Service Only if the Issue Is Fully Resolved
steps.instructions: Return the iQ200 to service only if aspiration is normal, no blockage error returns, QC is acceptable, and there are no leaks, abnormal sounds, unusual heat, or fluid handling concerns. Expected outcome: The device is safe and reliable for patient specimen processing.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory staff reported the Beckman Coulter iQ200 was generating repeated sample aspiration and flow cell blockage errors during urine microscopy testing.
CCR.Cause: Found heavy sediment in the affected specimen and visible air bubbles in the external sample fluid path after a recent rinse container change.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated fluid connections, verified rinse and waste setup, ran the approved cleaning and prime routine, completed QC successfully, and returned the analyzer to service.

helpfulDetails: Error message or alarm displayed
Whether issue affected one specimen or all specimens
Specimen appearance, sediment, foam, clots, or crystals
Rack and tube position checked
Probe exterior inspected
Cleaning or unclog routine completed
Rinse, reagent, and waste status verified
External tubing checked for kinks or leaks
Bubbles, fluid movement, or backup observed
QC or verification result after troubleshooting
Final device status

title: Beckman Coulter iQ200 Series Urine Analyzer - Poor Image Quality / Dirty Flow Cell
description: Troubleshooting poor urine particle images, dirty flow cell concerns, image artifacts, debris, or unclear microscopy images on the iQ200 Series.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the iQ200 Series analyzer is not actively processing patient specimens before pausing operation, inspecting sample areas, or performing cleaning. If patient testing is urgent, have laboratory staff use another validated analyzer or approved downtime process. Expected outcome: Patient testing is not compromised while image quality is being evaluated.

steps.title: Stop Testing and Confirm Result Integrity
steps.instructions: Ask laboratory staff whether any patient samples were processed while poor image quality was present. Confirm whether affected results were released, flagged, reviewed manually, or require repeat testing according to laboratory policy. Expected outcome: Questionable urine microscopy results are not reported without proper review.

steps.title: Verify the Image Quality Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask staff what they are seeing on the analyzer. Check whether the issue involves: Blurry images; Dark or low-contrast images; Excess debris in the image field; Streaking or bubbles; Repeated dirty flow cell message; High review rate; Unusual particle classification; Poor images on every sample; Poor images only on certain specimens. Expected outcome: The problem is confirmed as an image quality or flow cell cleanliness issue, not a LIS, sample ID, or operator review issue.

steps.title: Check for Recent Sample or Workflow Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether the issue started after a change in specimen handling, sample type, tube type, rack loading, cleaning schedule, reagent, or maintenance activity. Poor image quality can be caused by sediment-heavy samples, bubbles, carryover, improper mixing, or delayed processing. Expected outcome: Any workflow-related cause is identified before assuming analyzer failure.

steps.title: Inspect for Visible Fluid, Leaks, or Spills
steps.instructions: Look around the analyzer, sample area, waste area, and accessible fluid paths for leaks, dried urine residue, crystallized material, or spilled specimens. Do not continue testing if there is active leaking, unusual odor, fluid near electrical areas, or contamination around the analyzer. Expected outcome: The analyzer is safe to inspect and does not show an obvious external fluid problem.

steps.title: Confirm Proper Sample Preparation
steps.instructions: Verify with laboratory staff that samples are being mixed and loaded according to lab procedure. Settled sediment, clotted material, bubbles, or highly contaminated specimens can affect flow cell image quality. Expected outcome: Sample preparation issues are ruled out before device failure is suspected.

steps.title: Check for Bubbles or Foaming in Samples
steps.instructions: Ask staff if samples are foamy, recently shaken, or producing bubbles during aspiration. Bubbles passing through the flow cell may appear as image artifacts or interfere with particle recognition. Expected outcome: Bubble-related image issues are identified and corrected through proper sample handling.

steps.title: Verify Reagent, Diluent, and Fluid Supplies
steps.instructions: Check that required fluids are present, not expired, properly connected, and seated correctly. Confirm containers are not empty, kinked, blocked, or incorrectly installed. Expected outcome: The analyzer has the proper fluids needed for image flow and cleaning functions.

steps.title: Check Waste Container and Drain Path
steps.instructions: Verify the waste container is not full and that external waste tubing is not kinked, pinched, blocked, or positioned incorrectly. Poor drainage or backpressure can contribute to flow problems and dirty flow cell conditions. Expected outcome: Waste flow is not restricting analyzer operation.

steps.title: Inspect Accessible Tubing and Connections
steps.instructions: Check user-accessible tubing and external fluid connections for loose fittings, air gaps, crystallization, dried residue, or obvious blockage. Do not disassemble internal fluidic assemblies. Expected outcome: External fluid path issues are corrected without entering internal repair.

steps.title: Run the Approved Cleaning or Rinse Function
steps.instructions: If the analyzer provides an operator-accessible cleaning, rinse, or flow cell cleaning function, have qualified staff run the approved cycle according to site procedure. Do not use unapproved cleaning agents or improvised flushing methods. Expected outcome: Dirty flow cell or debris-related image quality issues improve after proper cleaning.

steps.title: Repeat Image Quality Check Using Known Acceptable Material
steps.instructions: After cleaning, have laboratory staff run appropriate QC, control material, or an approved verification sample. Review whether the images are clear, properly illuminated, and free of repeated debris, streaking, or bubble artifacts. Expected outcome: Image quality returns to acceptable performance before patient testing resumes.

steps.title: Check Whether the Issue Is Intermittent or Constant
steps.instructions: If poor image quality occurs only occasionally, compare affected samples for heavy sediment, bubbles, unusual color, high viscosity, or delayed testing. If every sample produces poor images, suspect a persistent flow cell, optics, fluidics, or imaging issue. Expected outcome: The failure pattern helps determine whether the cause is sample-related or analyzer-related.

steps.title: Review Error Messages and Maintenance History
steps.instructions: Check the analyzer display, event log, or staff reports for dirty flow cell, fluidics, camera, focus, illumination, or cleaning-related messages. Also review whether routine cleaning or maintenance is overdue. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting is supported by actual analyzer behavior and maintenance history.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: If cleaning and external checks do not resolve the issue, restart the analyzer only when no samples are processing and after confirming with laboratory staff. Expected outcome: Temporary software or initialization issues are cleared without interrupting active testing.

steps.title: Do Not Continue Patient Testing if Image Quality Remains Poor
steps.instructions: If images remain blurry, dirty, streaked, dark, or unreliable after external checks and approved cleaning, remove the analyzer from service. Expected outcome: Patient results are protected from unreliable microscopy image interpretation.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory reported poor urine particle image quality and repeated concern for a dirty flow cell on the Beckman Coulter iQ200 Series analyzer.
CCR.Cause: Found no external leaks or supply issues; image quality improved after approved cleaning/rinse cycle and verification testing.
CCR.Resolution: Performed external fluid, waste, tubing, and sample handling checks; cleaning cycle completed; QC images reviewed by lab and analyzer returned to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether patient results were affected
Exact image quality complaint
Dirty flow cell message present or not
Samples affected: all samples or specific specimens
QC result before and after cleaning
Cleaning or rinse cycle performed
Reagent and fluid levels checked
Waste container status
Tubing kinks, air gaps, or residue found
Any leaks, unusual odor, heat, or fluid near electronics
Error messages or event log details
Final device status

title: Beckman Coulter iQ200 Series Urine Analyzer - Particle Classification / Review Flag Issue
description: Troubleshooting particle classification errors, excessive review flags, abnormal image review, or questionable urine microscopy categorization on the iQ200 Series.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the iQ200 Series analyzer is not actively processing patient specimens before troubleshooting classification, imaging, or review flag concerns. If patient testing is urgent, have laboratory staff use another validated analyzer or approved manual microscopy process. Expected outcome: No questionable urine microscopy results are released while the issue is unresolved.

steps.title: Stop Reporting Questionable Results
steps.instructions: Ask laboratory staff whether any patient results were affected by abnormal particle classification or excessive review flags. Confirm whether those specimens require manual review, repeat testing, or manual microscopy per lab policy. Expected outcome: Results that may be misclassified are held or verified before release.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Classification Problem
steps.instructions: Ask staff what they are seeing. Check whether the issue involves: Excessive review flags; RBC, WBC, epithelial cell, bacteria, crystal, cast, yeast, or mucus misclassification; Images appearing blurry or poorly focused; High unclassified particle count; Frequent manual review requirement; Abnormal results that do not match expected specimen appearance; Problem only with certain specimen types. Expected outcome: The complaint is narrowed to classification, image quality, specimen quality, or workflow.

steps.title: Check Whether the Issue Is Isolated or Widespread
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue occurs on one sample, one shift, one operator, one rack, one specimen type, or all testing. If only one specimen is affected, suspect specimen quality, contamination, inadequate mixing, or unusual sediment. Expected outcome: A single-sample issue is separated from an analyzer-wide problem.

steps.title: Verify Specimen Handling and Mixing
steps.instructions: Confirm samples are fresh, properly labeled, adequately mixed, and processed within the laboratory’s allowed time window. Poorly mixed urine can cause uneven particle distribution and unexpected classification or review flags. Expected outcome: Specimen handling is not causing inconsistent particle results.

steps.title: Inspect Sample Containers and Racks
steps.instructions: Check that specimen containers are compatible, seated properly, filled to the required level, and free of exterior contamination. Verify racks are clean, undamaged, and loaded correctly. Expected outcome: Sample presentation to the analyzer is consistent and not interfering with aspiration or imaging.

steps.title: Check for Visible Sample Issues
steps.instructions: Ask lab staff whether affected samples are highly turbid, bloody, viscous, contaminated, or have heavy sediment. These conditions can increase review flags and make automated particle classification less reliable. Expected outcome: Abnormal specimen characteristics are recognized before assuming analyzer malfunction.

steps.title: Review Recent QC Results
steps.instructions: Confirm urine microscopy QC or applicable system QC has passed according to lab policy. If QC is failing or trending abnormally, stop patient testing and address QC before continuing. Expected outcome: The analyzer is not used for patient reporting unless QC status supports use.

steps.title: Check for Recent Reagent, Control, or Consumable Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether controls, consumables, wash solutions, diluents, or related supplies were recently changed. Confirm materials are correct, in date, properly stored, and installed as intended. Expected outcome: Consumable or material issues are ruled out as the cause of abnormal classification behavior.

steps.title: Inspect the Sample Aspiration Area Externally
steps.instructions: Look for obvious leaks, dried urine residue, salt buildup, debris, or sample carryover around user-accessible sample handling areas. Do not perform internal disassembly beyond approved operator-accessible cleaning. Expected outcome: External contamination or carryover sources are identified and corrected if accessible.

steps.title: Verify Routine Cleaning Was Completed
steps.instructions: Ask staff when the last required cleaning or maintenance was performed. Missed cleaning can contribute to carryover, debris, abnormal background, and increased review flags. Expected outcome: Maintenance status is confirmed and overdue user-level cleaning is completed per facility procedure.

steps.title: Review Image Quality With Laboratory Staff
steps.instructions: Have qualified lab personnel review stored images from affected specimens. Look for blurry images, excessive debris, particles at image edges, unusual background, or particles that appear inconsistent with the reported classification. Expected outcome: The issue is confirmed as image/classification related rather than a normal review process.

steps.title: Check for Persistent Background or Carryover Pattern
steps.instructions: If similar particles appear across unrelated samples, suspect carryover, contamination, fluid path residue, or imaging background issue. If the abnormal particles vary by sample, suspect specimen-related variation. Expected outcome: A repeating analyzer pattern is distinguished from specimen-to-specimen variability.

steps.title: Restart Only When Safe
steps.instructions: If no samples are in process, perform a normal shutdown and restart according to local procedure. Do not power cycle during active sample processing. Expected outcome: Temporary software or processing state issues are cleared without risking active patient results.

steps.title: Run QC or Known Acceptable Material After Restart
steps.instructions: Have laboratory staff run appropriate QC or approved check material after restart and cleaning. Confirm classification, imaging, and review flag behavior returns to expected operation. Expected outcome: Analyzer performance is verified before returning to routine patient testing.

steps.title: Check LIS or Middleware Interpretation Separately
steps.instructions: If the analyzer classification appears correct but the LIS displays unexpected flags, review whether middleware rules, result mapping, reference ranges, or auto-verification rules changed. Expected outcome: Analyzer classification issues are separated from downstream LIS or middleware flagging problems.

steps.title: Return to Service Only If Results Are Reliable
steps.instructions: If QC passes, image quality is acceptable, flags return to expected frequency, and laboratory staff confirm results are clinically reviewable, return the analyzer to service. Expected outcome: The device is used only after result integrity has been confirmed.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory staff reported excessive review flags and questionable particle classification on the Beckman Coulter iQ200 Series urine analyzer.
CCR.Cause: Found QC acceptable but multiple patient samples showed increased unclassified particles after missed user-level cleaning and visible residue near the sample handling area.
CCR.Resolution: Removed analyzer from active testing, had staff complete approved cleaning, restarted the analyzer, verified acceptable QC and image review performance, and returned unit to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether patient results were held, repeated, or manually reviewed
Specific particles being misclassified
Review flag frequency or pattern
QC status before and after troubleshooting
Whether issue affected one sample or all samples
Specimen appearance: turbid, bloody, viscous, heavy sediment
Cleaning or maintenance status
Consumables or controls recently changed
Image quality observations
Any visible residue, leaks, or carryover signs
LIS or middleware flagging behavior
Final device status

title: Beckman Coulter iQ200 Series Urine Analyzer - Rack Handler / Sampler Movement Fault
description: Troubleshooting iQ200 rack handler, sampler movement, rack loading, transport, or positioning faults caused by external or user-accessible issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the iQ200 is not actively processing patient urine specimens before touching the rack handler, sampler area, or transport path. If testing is needed immediately, have laboratory staff use another validated analyzer or follow the facility’s approved downtime process. Expected outcome: No patient specimen is interrupted, lost, or reported from a device with an unresolved sampler fault.

steps.title: Stop Additional Rack Loading
steps.instructions: Ask staff not to load additional racks until the movement fault is understood. Confirm whether any specimens were in process when the fault occurred and whether results were completed, aborted, or need repeat testing per lab policy. Expected outcome: Incomplete or questionable urine results are not released.

steps.title: Verify the Exact Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask staff what they observed. Check whether the issue involves: Rack not advancing; Rack jammed in the loader; Sampler arm not moving; Rack handler alarm or motion fault; Specimen not reaching the sampling position; Intermittent rack movement; Failure after rack loading, cleaning, or maintenance. Expected outcome: The issue is confirmed as a rack handler or sampler movement problem, not a barcode, LIS, optics, or chemistry result issue.

steps.title: Inspect the Rack Loading Area
steps.instructions: Check the rack input area for misloaded racks, tilted tubes, loose caps, labels, spilled urine, or debris. Remove any visible obstruction without forcing the rack handler or sampler mechanism. Expected outcome: Rack movement is no longer blocked by an external obstruction.

steps.title: Check Rack Orientation and Rack Type
steps.instructions: Confirm the correct rack type is being used and that it is loaded in the proper orientation. Verify that sample tubes are seated correctly and are not too tall, loose, uncapped incorrectly, or interfering with movement. Expected outcome: The rack can sit flat and move through the handler without binding.

steps.title: Check for Sample Tube or Label Interference
steps.instructions: Inspect tubes for peeling barcode labels, excessive label overlap, loose secondary labels, or caps that may catch on the sampler path. Replace or relabel problem tubes per lab procedure before retesting. Expected outcome: Tube and label condition does not interfere with rack movement or sample positioning.

steps.title: Inspect for Spills or Residue
steps.instructions: Look for urine, control material, dried residue, or crystallized buildup around the rack path, sample area, and accessible transport surfaces. If contamination is present, have the area cleaned according to the operator-level cleaning procedure and allow it to dry before testing. Expected outcome: The rack handler and sampler area are clean enough for normal movement.

steps.title: Check Waste and Nearby Consumable Areas
steps.instructions: Verify that nearby waste containers, covers, trays, and accessible doors are properly seated. Some analyzer movement faults can occur when adjacent covers, waste components, or access panels are not fully closed or detected. Expected outcome: All user-accessible components are seated and not blocking the sampler path.

steps.title: Confirm the Analyzer Is Level and Stable
steps.instructions: Make sure the iQ200 is on a stable surface and has not been bumped, shifted, or placed where racks can drag against nearby objects. Check that racks are not contacting walls, other instruments, cords, or benchtop items during loading. Expected outcome: Rack movement is not restricted by analyzer placement or surrounding objects.

steps.title: Check for Unusual Sounds or Mechanical Resistance
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether they heard grinding, repeated clicking, slipping, or motor movement without rack travel. Do not force the rack handler if resistance is felt. Expected outcome: Obvious mechanical binding or drive failure is identified before repeated restart attempts.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe
steps.instructions: If no rack is trapped in a way that creates risk, restart the analyzer according to normal site procedure. Allow the system to complete startup checks without racks loaded unless the analyzer prompts otherwise. Expected outcome: A temporary motion or initialization error clears without bypassing safety checks.

steps.title: Run an Operator-Level Function Check
steps.instructions: After cleaning and clearing the rack path, have qualified laboratory staff or Clinical Engineering run an appropriate non-patient check using an empty rack, control material, or facility-approved test process. Do not use patient specimens to prove a movement issue is resolved. Expected outcome: Rack movement and sampler positioning operate normally before the analyzer returns to patient testing.

steps.title: Review Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether the issue started after: Rack cleaning; Sample tube type change; Barcode label change; Relocation of the analyzer; Preventive maintenance; Spill cleanup; Software or workflow change. Expected outcome: A recent external change is identified and corrected before internal failure is suspected.

steps.title: Return to Service Only After Successful Verification
steps.instructions: If the rack handler moves normally, no alarms return, and the analyzer passes the facility’s required operational or QC checks, document the findings and return the device to service. Expected outcome: The iQ200 is safe for use and the movement fault is resolved.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory staff reported that the Beckman Coulter iQ200 stopped during sample processing with a rack handler movement fault and would not advance the rack.
CCR.Cause: Found one sample tube with a peeling barcode label contacting the rack path and preventing smooth rack movement.
CCR.Resolution: Removed affected rack from use, had staff relabel the specimen per lab policy, cleaned accessible residue from the rack loading area, verified rack movement with a non-patient check, and returned the analyzer to service.

helpfulDetails: Exact rack handler or sampler error message
Whether a patient specimen was in process
Whether results were completed, aborted, or repeated
Rack position when the fault occurred
Rack type and tube type used
Barcode label condition
Evidence of spills, residue, or debris
Covers, trays, and waste areas checked
Any unusual clicking, grinding, or motor noise
Whether the analyzer was recently moved or cleaned
Power cycle result
QC or operational verification result
Final device status

title: Beckman Coulter iQ200 Series Urine Analyzer - Workstation / Database / LIS Communication Failure
description: Troubleshooting iQ200 workstation, database, host, LIS, middleware, or result transmission failures caused by external communication issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the iQ200 Series analyzer and workstation are not actively processing or releasing patient results before restarting systems, disconnecting network cables, or changing communication settings. If testing must continue, have laboratory staff follow the approved downtime process or use another validated analyzer. Expected outcome: Patient testing and result reporting are protected during troubleshooting.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Communication Failure
steps.instructions: Ask laboratory staff what specifically is failing. Check whether the issue involves: Results not reaching the LIS; Results stuck at the workstation; Workstation not receiving analyzer data; Middleware rejection; Database error or slow workstation response; Host connection down; Worklist download failure; Manual result transmission failure; Intermittent result upload delays. Expected outcome: The issue is identified as workstation, database, analyzer-to-workstation, or LIS/middleware communication related.

steps.title: Protect Result Integrity
steps.instructions: Confirm whether any patient results were generated during the failure. Ask staff whether results are visible on the iQ200 workstation and whether they were manually documented or held pending LIS recovery. Expected outcome: No urine microscopy or chemistry result is lost, duplicated, or released without proper verification.

steps.title: Check Workstation Power and Basic Operation
steps.instructions: Verify the iQ200 workstation is powered on, logged in, and responsive. Look for frozen screens, Windows errors, database warnings, or application lockups. If the workstation is frozen, coordinate with lab staff before restarting so pending results are not lost. Expected outcome: The workstation is available and able to run the iQ200 software.

steps.title: Confirm the iQ200 Application Is Running Correctly
steps.instructions: Check whether the iQ200 software is open and connected to the analyzer. Look for communication status indicators, host status messages, analyzer connection warnings, or database-related alerts. Expected outcome: The workstation software is running normally, or a software/database issue is clearly identified.

steps.title: Check Analyzer-to-Workstation Communication
steps.instructions: Verify that the analyzer and workstation connection is intact. Check external data cables, network patch cables, serial cables, or USB/communication adapters depending on the installed configuration. Reseat only external connections that are safe and accessible. Expected outcome: Loose or disconnected analyzer-to-workstation communication cabling is corrected.

steps.title: Inspect Network Connections
steps.instructions: Verify the workstation network cable is fully seated at the PC and wall jack or network switch. Check for link lights at the Ethernet port if visible. If wireless or network adapters are used in the site setup, confirm the expected connection is active. Expected outcome: The workstation has an active physical network connection.

steps.title: Confirm Other Devices Are Communicating
steps.instructions: Ask whether other lab instruments are sending results to the LIS. If multiple devices are affected, the problem is more likely network, middleware, or LIS related rather than isolated to the iQ200. Expected outcome: The failure is narrowed to either the iQ200 system or a broader interface issue.

steps.title: Check for Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Ask staff or IT whether there were recent changes to: LIS or middleware; Network ports or VLANs; Workstation login credentials; Antivirus or Windows updates; Instrument interface settings; IP address assignments; Database maintenance; Lab workflow or accession format. Expected outcome: Recent changes are identified before assuming hardware failure.

steps.title: Verify Workstation Network Identity
steps.instructions: Confirm the workstation has the expected network connection and is not showing “No Internet,” “Unidentified network,” or a disconnected adapter status. If site policy allows, coordinate with IT or LIS support to verify the workstation IP address, hostname, and network reachability. Expected outcome: The workstation is recognized on the correct hospital or lab network.

steps.title: Check Host/LIS Status From the Workstation
steps.instructions: Review the iQ200 software communication or host interface status screen if available to Clinical Engineering. Confirm whether the host is shown as connected, disconnected, idle, offline, or in error. Expected outcome: Host communication status is confirmed before changing anything.

steps.title: Attempt a Controlled Manual Transmission
steps.instructions: With lab approval, select a non-critical or already-verified result and attempt manual transmission according to site procedure. Do not repeatedly send patient results without confirming whether the LIS already received them. Expected outcome: Manual send either succeeds, confirming recovery, or fails with a useful error pattern.

steps.title: Check for Database or Storage Symptoms
steps.instructions: Look for signs that the workstation database may be affected: Application opens slowly; Results will not save; Search functions fail; Database error messages appear; Workstation freezes during result review; Disk space warning appears; Results queue will not clear. Expected outcome: The issue is identified as possible database/workstation performance trouble rather than only LIS communication.

steps.title: Check Date and Time
steps.instructions: Verify the workstation date and time are correct. Incorrect system time can affect result timestamps, middleware handling, worklists, audit trails, or interface acceptance. Expected outcome: Result timing and interface data are not being rejected due to incorrect time settings.

steps.title: Restart Only When Safe
steps.instructions: If external checks do not resolve the issue and lab staff confirm no active patient processing is occurring, perform a controlled restart of the iQ200 application or workstation according to facility procedure. Avoid powering down the analyzer or workstation while results are actively processing or transmitting. Expected outcome: Temporary application, communication, or workstation lockups are cleared without compromising patient data.

steps.title: Verify Recovery
steps.instructions: After restart or correction, confirm: Workstation software opens normally; Analyzer status is normal; Pending results are still available; Manual or automatic result transmission works; LIS receives the expected result; No duplicate result was created; Lab staff confirm normal workflow. If the issue is resolved, document the findings and return the system to service. Expected outcome: iQ200 communication is restored and result integrity is confirmed.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory reported the Beckman Coulter iQ200 workstation was not transmitting urine microscopy results to the LIS.
CCR.Cause: Found the workstation network cable loose at the wall jack and host communication status offline.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the network connection, confirmed link activity, performed a controlled manual result transmission with lab approval, and verified LIS receipt before returning the system to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the analyzer was actively processing specimens
Whether results were stored locally on the workstation
Exact workstation, database, host, or LIS error message
Analyzer-to-workstation connection status
Network cable and wall jack checked
Ethernet link lights present or absent
Manual send attempted
Whether LIS received duplicate, delayed, or missing results
Whether other lab instruments were affected
Recent IT, LIS, middleware, or Windows changes
Workstation date and time verified
Application restart or workstation reboot performed
Final device status
